summary: English satirical novelist known for Brideshead Revisited, A Handful of Dust, Scoop, and Decline and Fall. Catholic convert whose first name led to gender confusion.

works.name: Brideshead Revisited
works.description: Waugh's best-known novel about the painter Charles Ryder's friendship with the wealthy Catholic Flyte/Marchmain family at Oxford. Sebastian Flyte carries the teddy bear Aloysius (inspired by John Betjeman's Archibald Ormsby-Gore). Sebastian becomes an alcoholic and retires to a Tunisian monastery. Ryder has an affair with Julia Flyte. Lord Marchmain makes the sign of the cross on his deathbed. Rex Mottram is Julia's politically ambitious Canadian husband. Anthony Blanche shouts passages from The Waste Land from a window. Begins during Eights Week rowing races. Ryder's epilogue use of 'an ancient, newly learned form of words' implies his conversion to Catholicism. Waugh's affair with Alastair Graham at Oxford inspired Sebastian. Paul Pennyfeather from Decline and Fall and Ryder both attended Oxford.

clues.clue: Charles Ryder befriends Sebastian Flyte at Oxford; Sebastian carries teddy bear Aloysius
examples: features the alcoholic Sebastian Flyte, who befriends the painter Charles Ryder
this character breaks a tiny bone in his foot while playing croquet and carries around the teddy bear Aloysius

clues.clue: Lord Marchmain makes the sign of the cross on his deathbed; Catholic themes
examples: a man who had abandoned his faith makes the sign of the cross on his deathbed to return the Catholic fold

clues.clue: Sebastian retires to a Tunisian monastery
examples: this character eventually becomes a porter at a Tunisian monastery

clues.clue: Julia Flyte married Rex Mottram; Ryder's affair with Julia
examples: Julia marries Canadian Rex Mottram rather than the man commissioned to paint for the Marchmains

clues.clue: Teddy bear Aloysius inspired by Betjeman's Archibald Ormsby-Gore

clues.clue: Anthony Blanche shouts Waste Land passages from a window
examples: Anthony Blanche shouts passages from The Waste Land out of a window

clues.clue: Ryder's use of 'ancient, newly learned form of words' in epilogue implies Catholic conversion
examples: Ryder's use of 'an ancient, newly learned form of words' implies that he has since taken this action

works.name: A Handful of Dust
works.description: Tony Last leaves Hetton Abbey after divorcing Brenda (who has an affair with John Beaver). He travels to South America where he is captured by Mr. Todd and forced to read Dickens novels aloud indefinitely. Brenda reacts with relief when learning her son (not her lover with the same first name) was killed in a riding accident. The title comes from a T. S. Eliot line in The Waste Land: 'I will show you fear in a handful of dust.' Tony plays 'animal snap' with his son. Dr. Messinger falls over a waterfall.

clues.clue: Tony Last captured in South America, forced to read Dickens aloud to Mr. Todd indefinitely
examples: Tony Last is forced to read Dickens aloud forever
a man trying to get a divorce is held captive to read Dickens

clues.clue: Brenda relieved that son (not lover with same first name) died in riding accident
examples: A woman reacts with relief when she learns that her son has been killed in a riding accident instead of her lover with the same first name

clues.clue: Title from T. S. Eliot's Waste Land; epigraph from 'The Burial of the Dead'
examples: This author used a line from Eliot to title his novel A Handful of Dust

clues.clue: Tony plays 'animal snap' with his son at Hetton Abbey
examples: a man who plays the card game 'animal snap' with his son

works.name: Scoop
works.description: A novel inspired by Waugh's time covering the Second Italo-Abyssinian War for the Daily Mail. The Daily Beast sends the wrong man named Boot (nature columnist William Boot instead of novelist John Boot) to cover a civil war in the fictional country of Ishmaelia. Boot's columns feature purple prose like 'Feather-footed through the plashy fen passes the questing vole.' Boot buys rocks from a woman at the Pension Dressler and gives her a collapsible canoe. The editor Mr. Salter says 'Up to a point' to Lord Copper. Boot convinces his uncle to impersonate him at a banquet. The novel is said to be based on future Conservative Lord William Deedes.

clues.clue: Daily Beast sends wrong Boot to cover Ishmaelia; William Boot is nature columnist
examples: the Daily Beast sends the wrong man named Boot to cover a civil war in Ishmaelia
a man who writes the 'Lush Places' column for The Daily Beast

clues.clue: Inspired by Waugh's time as war correspondent in Abyssinia for Daily Mail
examples: This man covered the Second Italo-Abyssinian War for the Daily Mail

works.name: Vile Bodies
works.description: Waugh's second and 'most overtly modernist' novel, satirizing the 'Bright Young Things.' Adam Fenwick-Symes seeks to marry Nina Blount and works as gossip columnist Mr. Chatterbox. Agatha Runcible dies after a car racing accident. The novel was Waugh's follow-up to Decline and Fall.

clues.clue: Satire of 'Bright Young Things'; Adam Fenwick-Symes; Agatha Runcible dies from car racing
examples: a satire on the 'Bright Young Things' about Adam Fenwick-Symes
the alcoholic Agatha Runcible is put in a nursing home and dies due to her involvement in one of these events

works.name: Other Works and Biographical
works.description: Decline and Fall features Paul Pennyfeather expelled from fictional Scone College after the Bollinger Club dares him to run without pants. The Loved One satirizes Hollywood funeral culture with Mr. Joyboy and Dennis Barlow. Black Mischief features the African nation of Azania ruined by Oxford-educated Emperor Seth. The Sword of Honour trilogy (including Unconditional Surrender and Men at Arms) follows Guy Crouchback in WWII; Cyril Connolly is satirized as Everard Spruce. The Ordeal of Gilbert Pinfold recounts Waugh's hallucinatory voyage. Love Among the Ruins is a dystopian novella with Miles Plastic. Waugh's first name caused gender confusion (a reviewer assumed his Rossetti biography was by a woman); he married a woman with the same name. His brother Alec wrote The Loom of Youth. Waugh quipped about removing 'the one part of Randolph [Churchill] which was not malignant.' He was a Catholic convert and close to Nancy Mitford.

clues.clue: Catholic convert whose affair with Alastair Graham at Oxford inspired Sebastian Flyte
examples: This Catholic convert's affair with Alastair Graham at Oxford inspired one of his best-known characters

clues.clue: Ambiguous first name; married a woman named Evelyn; brother Alec wrote The Loom of Youth
examples: This author's ambiguous first name led a reviewer to assume his biography was written by a woman

clues.clue: Decline and Fall: Paul Pennyfeather expelled from Scone College, Bollinger Club
examples: A character is expelled from the fictional Scone College after the Bollinger Club dares him to run across the courtyard without pants

clues.clue: The Ordeal of Gilbert Pinfold: semi-autobiographical 'mad book' about hallucinations
examples: This author's voyage to Ceylon was interrupted by hallucinations that he recounted in The Ordeal of Gilbert Pinfold

clues.clue: Son Auberon Waugh co-founded Bad Sex in Fiction Award at Literary Review
examples: As editor of the Literary Review, he co-founded the Bad Sex in Fiction Award

comprehensive_summary:
Evelyn Waugh is an English satirical novelist whose quizbowl giveaway is invariably Brideshead Revisited, the novel about painter Charles Ryder's friendship with the wealthy Catholic Marchmain/Flyte family at Oxford. Sebastian Flyte carries the teddy bear Aloysius (inspired by John Betjeman's bear Archibald Ormsby-Gore), becomes an alcoholic, and retires to a Tunisian monastery. Lord Marchmain makes the sign of the cross on his deathbed. Julia Flyte marries the politically ambitious Canadian Rex Mottram.

A Handful of Dust is the second most important work: Tony Last leaves Hetton Abbey after his wife Brenda's affair with John Beaver, travels to South America, and is captured by Mr. Todd, who forces him to read Dickens aloud indefinitely. The title comes from T. S. Eliot's Waste Land. Brenda's relief that her dead son (not her same-named lover) was killed is a key power clue.

Scoop satirizes war journalism: the Daily Beast sends the wrong Boot (nature columnist William, not novelist John) to cover Ishmaelia. 'Feather-footed through the plashy fen passes the questing vole' is Boot's purple prose. Vile Bodies satirizes the 'Bright Young Things' with Adam Fenwick-Symes and Agatha Runcible.

Other works include Decline and Fall (Paul Pennyfeather expelled from Scone College), The Loved One (Hollywood funeral satire), Black Mischief (Azania), and the Sword of Honour trilogy. Waugh was a Catholic convert whose Oxford affair with Alastair Graham inspired Sebastian. His ambiguous first name caused gender confusion, and he married a woman also named Evelyn.

cards.front: Novel: Charles Ryder befriends the wealthy Catholic Flyte family at Oxford; Sebastian carries the teddy bear Aloysius and later retires to a Tunisian monastery
Novel: Lord Marchmain makes the sign of the cross on his deathbed, returning to the Catholic fold after living with his mistress Cara in Venice
Novel: Tony Last is captured in South America and forced to read Dickens aloud to Mr. Todd indefinitely; titled from a T. S. Eliot line
Novel: a woman reacts with relief when her son (not her lover with the same first name) is killed in a riding accident at Hetton Abbey
Novel: the Daily Beast sends the wrong man named Boot to cover a civil war in Ishmaelia; 'Feather-footed through the plashy fen passes the questing vole'
Novel: satire of the 'Bright Young Things'; Adam Fenwick-Symes works as gossip columnist Mr. Chatterbox; Agatha Runcible dies from a car racing accident
Novel: Paul Pennyfeather is expelled from fictional Scone College after the Bollinger Club dares him to run across the courtyard without pants
